title: JSC370 Midterm Project
text: GitHub Repository

title: JSC370 Midterm Project
text: A snapshot of the data

title: JSC370 Midterm Project
text:
Summary Statistics
Table 1. Descriptive Statistics of the Dataset
Table 1 reports descriptive statistics for the main variables used in the analysis. Over the sample period, both the S&P 500 and the Dow Jones indices display very large ranges between their minimum and maximum values, reflecting the long time span of the dataset and the substantial growth of U.S. equity markets since 2000.
The Volatility Index (VIX) has an average level of approximately 19.8, but its maximum exceeds 80 during periods of extreme financial stress. Such spikes occur during major market disruptions, including the 2008 global financial crisis and the COVID-19 market collapse in 2020. Oil prices also show substantial variation over time, including the unprecedented negative prices briefly observed during the 2020 oil market shock. The dates where these two variables reach extreme or unusual values will be examined below.
Daily returns of the S&P 500 and Dow Jones are centered close to zero, with a standard deviation of 1.2%. This is consistent with the well-known statistical property that financial returns fluctuate around a small mean but exhibit considerable volatility.


High-Volatility Dates
Table 2. High-Volatility Months With VIX > 40
Table 2 reports months in which the Volatility Index exceeded 40 on at least one trading day, along with the number of such days within each month. Extreme volatility events are highly concentrated in a small number of crisis periods rather than being evenly distributed across the sample.
The largest clusters occur during the global financial crisis of 2008–2009. For example, October 2008 and March 2009 both contain more than twenty trading days with VIX values above 40, indicating sustained market stress during this period. Another major cluster appears in early 2020, corresponding to the market turmoil associated with the COVID-19 pandemic, when March and April 2020 contain 18 and 14 extreme-volatility days respectively.
Outside of these crisis episodes, extreme volatility occurs only sporadically, such as after the September 11 attacks in New York City, during the European debt crisis in 2011, or in response to the United States tariff policy in April 2025. This pattern highlights that extreme volatility events are rare and tend to be associated with major macroeconomic shocks.



Negative Oil Price Dates
Table 3. Negative Oil Price Dates
The only occurence of negative oil prices in the dataset is on April 20, 2020, when the West Texas Intermediate crude oil futures contract for May delivery briefly traded below zero, mainly driven by the unprecedented collapse in demand during the COVID-19 pandemic and a supply glut.

title: JSC370 Midterm Project
text:
Stock Market Indices Over Time
Figure 1. S&P 500 and Dow Jones Index Over Time
Figure 1 illustrates the historical evolution of the two major U.S. stock market indices included in the dataset. Both indices show strong long-term upward trends, reflecting economic expansion, corporate earnings growth, and increasing market capitalization over the past several decades.
Despite this overall growth trend, several sharp market declines are visible. Notable drops occur during the 2000 dot-com crash, the 2008 global financial crisis, and the market shock associated with the COVID-19 pandemic in early 2020. The S&P 500 and Dow Jones move closely together throughout the sample period, suggesting that they capture similar underlying market conditions and respond similarly to macroeconomic shocks. In fact, as the heat map below will show, the two indices have a correlation as high as 0.99, which is expected given that they both represent broad measures of U.S. equity market performance.

title: JSC370 Midterm Project
text:
Market Volatility Over Time
Figure 2. Market Volatility (VIX) Over Time
Figure 2 plots the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX), which is commonly interpreted as a measure of market uncertainty or investor fear. The index remains relatively low during stable market conditions but spikes sharply during financial crises and periods of heightened uncertainty.
Large spikes are clearly visible during the 2008 financial crisis and again in 2020 during the global pandemic. Outside of crisis periods, the VIX generally remains between approximately 10 and 25, indicating relatively calm market conditions. These patterns highlight the role of volatility as an important indicator of financial stress.

title: JSC370 Midterm Project
text:
Distribution of Daily Stock Returns
Figure 3. Distribution of Daily Returns
The distribution of daily returns for both the S&P 500 and the Dow Jones indices is centered near zero, with most observations concentrated around small positive or negative values. However, the distribution exhibits heavy tails, meaning that large positive and negative returns occasionally occur. This pattern is typical of financial return series and reflects the presence of extreme market events and volatility clustering. One notable feature is that the two distributions appear to be almost identical, which is consistent with the very high correlation between the two indices. This suggests that they are influenced by similar underlying market factors and respond similarly to macroeconomic conditions, and that we may only model one of the two indices in subsequent analyses without losing much information.

title: JSC370 Midterm Project
text:
Relationship Between Volatility and Returns
Figure 4. Relationship Between Market Volatility (VIX) and Stock Returns
The fitted regression lines suggest a weak negative relationship between market volatility and daily returns. As VIX values increase, the average daily return tends to decrease slightly, indicating that higher levels of market uncertainty are often associated with poorer market performance.
Most observations are concentrated at lower volatility levels , where daily returns tend to cluster closely around zero. As volatility increases beyond this range, the dispersion of returns becomes noticeably wider. High VIX levels are associated with both large positive and large negative returns, indicating that extreme volatility corresponds to greater uncertainty and larger market movements in both directions.


Difference in Returns During Extreme Volatility
To examine whether extreme volatility periods are associated with different stock market performance, we compare mean S&P 500 daily returns between high-volatility days (VIX > 40) and normal days.
Null hypothesis (H₀):
The mean S&P 500 return is the same during high-volatility periods and normal periods.
Alternative hypothesis (H₁):
The mean S&P 500 return is lower during high-volatility periods than during normal periods.
Table 3. Results of Two-Sample t-Test On Difference In Mean Return
The p-value is 0.00256, which is less than the common significance level of 0.05. This indicates that we reject the null hypothesis and conclude that mean S&P 500 returns are significantly lower during high-volatility periods (VIX > 40) compared to normal periods, as expected based on the negative relationship observed in the scatter plot.

title: JSC370 Midterm Project
section: Relationship Between Treasury Yields and Returns
text:
Relationship Between Treasury Yields and Returns
Figure 5. Relationship Between 10-Year Treasury Yield and Stock Returns
The fitted regression line appears nearly flat, indicating a very weak linear relationship between long-term interest rates and short-term stock returns. This suggests that daily fluctuations in equity markets are not strongly driven by the level of the 10-year Treasury yield. While interest rates influence valuation models through discount rates and cost of capital, their immediate effect on daily market movements appears limited.
Most observations cluster around small positive or negative returns, with occasional outliers reflecting periods of heightened market volatility. These extreme values likely correspond to major macroeconomic announcements, monetary policy shifts, or unexpected financial market shocks. Overall, the evidence suggests that although Treasury yields are a key macroeconomic indicator, their short-term relationship with daily stock returns is relatively weak.

title: JSC370 Midterm Project
section: Relationship Between Oil Prices and Returns
text:
Relationship Between Oil Prices and Returns
Figure 6. Relationship Between Oil Price and Stock Returns
A concentration of observations occurs when oil prices are between roughly $40 and $100 per barrel, where most market activity took place during the sample period. Returns remain centered around zero in this range. However, it is possible that although the two aggregated indices’ return is independent from oil prices, there are still stocks, when evaluated on their own, may be impacted and should be further studied.

title: JSC370 Midterm Project
text:
Correlation Between Market Indicators
Figure 7. Correlation Heatmap of Market Indicators
Strong positive correlations are visible among major equity indices. In particular, the S&P 500 and Dow Jones indices exhibit near-perfect correlation, which is expected because both indices track the performance of large U.S. companies and tend to respond similarly to overall market conditions.
Several macroeconomic variables demonstrate meaningful relationships with financial conditions. Industrial production and inflation exhibit moderate positive correlation, suggesting that stronger economic activity often coincides with higher price pressures. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate tends to be negatively correlated with industrial production, consistent with the economic cycle in which stronger output is associated with lower unemployment.
Financial stress indicators show distinct patterns as well. The Volatility Index and credit spread measures tend to move together, reflecting periods when investors demand higher compensation for risk. These variables generally show negative correlations with equity indices, indicating that higher market uncertainty or credit risk is often associated with weaker stock market performance.
In contrast, correlations between many macroeconomic indicators and daily stock returns (e.g., S&P 500 return and Dow Jones return) are relatively weak. This suggests that while macroeconomic variables influence long-term market trends, they may not provide strong explanatory power for short-term daily fluctuations in equity returns.


Statistical Significance of Correlations
Table 4 reports Pearson correlation tests between lagged macro-financial indicators and daily S&P 500 returns. The lag structure ensures that predictors reflect information available on the previous trading day, avoiding look-ahead bias.
The results indicate that most predictors have extremely weak correlations with daily stock returns. Lagged volatility (VIX) shows a small positive correlation with returns (r = 0.038, p = 0.002), while the 10-year Treasury yield shows a small negative correlation (r = −0.031, p = 0.014). Although these relationships are statistically significant, their magnitudes are extremely small.
Other predictors—including the USD index, oil prices, credit spreads, and consumer sentiment—show no statistically significant correlation with daily returns. Overall, these results suggest that macro-financial indicators have limited ability to explain short-term movements in stock market returns.



Predictive Regression
\(R^2 = 0.005\), Dependent Variable is Next Day S&P 500 Return
The predictive regression examines whether lagged macro-financial indicators help explain daily S&P 500 returns. The overall model is statistically significant (F = 5.53, p < 0.001), but the explanatory power is very small (\(R^2 = 0.005\)): the predictors explain only about 0.5% of the variation in daily returns.
Among the predictors, lagged market volatility, the 10-year Treasury yield, the USD index, and credit spreads are statistically significant. Lagged volatility has a positive coefficient, while the 10-year yield, USD index, and credit spreads show negative relationships with returns. Oil prices show weak evidence of a relationship, and consumer sentiment does not appear to be statistically significant.
Overall, while some macro-financial indicators are statistically related to next-day returns, their effects are small in magnitude.

title: JSC370 Midterm Project
text:
Results So Far
The results so far suggest that the collected indicators contain some statistically detectable information, but their ability to explain daily stock returns is very limited. The descriptive analysis shows that the sample includes clear episodes of market stress, especially during the 2008 financial crisis and the 2020 COVID-19 market collapse. These crisis periods are visible in both the stock index plots and the volatility series, and the VIX exceeds 40 only during a relatively small number of months concentrated around major shocks. This indicates that extreme volatility is not a common market state, but when it occurs, it is associated with severe disruptions in financial conditions.
The return distribution also shows that daily stock returns are centered close to zero and have heavy tails. This means that most days involve small changes, but large positive and negative returns occur more frequently than would be expected under a normal distribution. This feature already suggests that predicting daily returns will be difficult, because the series is noisy and driven partly by rare but important shocks.
The relationship plots provide a mixed picture. The VIX has the clearest visual relationship with stock returns: as volatility rises, average returns tend to become lower, and the dispersion of returns becomes wider. The t-test confirms that mean S&P 500 returns are significantly lower on days with extreme volatility (VIX > 40) than on normal days. In contrast, the scatter plots for the 10-year Treasury yield and oil prices show much weaker patterns. The fitted lines are nearly flat, and the observations remain widely dispersed across the range of these variables. This suggests that these indicators may not have strong direct linear relationships with daily returns at a one-day horizon.
The correlation analysis leads to a similar conclusion. Lagged volatility and lagged Treasury yields show statistically significant correlations with next-day S&P 500 returns, but the magnitudes of these correlations are extremely small. Other lagged predictors, such as the USD index, oil prices, credit spreads, and consumer sentiment, do not show statistically significant simple correlations. This means that while some variables appear related to returns, the strength of those relationships is weak in practical terms.
The predictive regression produces the same general finding. The model is statistically significant overall, which indicates that the predictors jointly contain some information about next-day returns. However, the explanatory power is very low, with an \(R^2\) of approximately 0.005. In other words, the model explains only about 0.5% of the variation in daily S&P 500 returns. Lagged volatility, the 10-year Treasury yield, the USD index, and credit spreads are statistically significant in the regression, while oil prices show only weak evidence and consumer sentiment is not significant. Taken together, these results suggest that daily macro-financial indicators may have limited short-run predictive content, but they are far from sufficient for strong return prediction on their own.
At this stage, my answer to the original research question is therefore conservative: daily macro-financial indicators appear to have weak predictive relationships with future stock market returns, but their practical predictive power is very small when tested using simple linear methods. This does not mean the indicators are useless. Instead, it suggests that the relationships may be nonlinear, state-dependent, or only visible when variables are combined in more flexible predictive models.

title: JSC370 Midterm Project
text:
Plan for the Final Project

Additional data collection
Although the current dataset covers a long historical period (2020–2026) and includes several major macro-financial indicators, it is still limited in a few important ways. First, the set of predictors currently focuses primarily on broad macroeconomic variables and market indicators. While these variables capture overall economic conditions, they may not fully reflect the information that investors use to form short-term expectations about market movements. In particular, the dataset does not yet include variables related to monetary policy expectations, credit market stress beyond simple spreads, or other financial market indicators that may contain predictive information.
For the final project, I therefore plan to expand the dataset by collecting several additional variables from the FRED API and other financial data sources. Possible additions include:

Term spread indicators such as the difference between the 10-year and 3-month Treasury yields, which are widely used measures of economic expectations.
Corporate credit market indicators, such as additional bond spreads or financial stress indices.
Monetary policy expectation measures, such as futures-implied policy rates or alternative interest-rate series available through FRED.
Market liquidity indicators, if available through accessible APIs.
Additional equity market indicators, such as trading volume or other volatility measures derived from market data.

These variables may capture different dimensions of financial conditions and could improve predictive performance when used in machine learning models.
Another limitation of the current dataset is that it uses only daily frequency data. Some macroeconomic variables, such as inflation, unemployment, and industrial production, are naturally measured monthly. In the current dataset these series were forward-filled to daily frequency to align them with market data. While this approach allows the variables to be included in the analysis, it may reduce the effective information content of those predictors at a daily horizon. In the final project, I will therefore carefully examine whether models perform better when using features derived from higher-frequency financial variables (such as volatility, credit spreads, and market momentum) rather than relying heavily on low-frequency macroeconomic indicators.



Planned feature engineering
Before fitting machine learning models, I will expand the predictor set beyond the simple one-day lag structure used in the midterm. The final feature set will include:

Multiple lags of each predictor
Instead of using only lag 1, I will create lag 1, lag 3, lag 5, and lag 10 versions for variables such as VIX, Treasury yields, USD index, oil prices, and credit spreads. This will allow the models to detect delayed effects rather than assuming that all predictive information enters only one day later.
Rolling averages and moving-window summaries
For variables such as VIX, oil prices, and Treasury yields, I will calculate 5-day, 10-day, and 20-day rolling means. These features may capture persistent market conditions better than single-day values.
Rolling volatility of stock returns
I will compute 5-day, 10-day, and 20-day rolling standard deviations of S&P 500 and Dow Jones returns. This will provide a direct measure of recent market turbulence and may improve prediction compared to using the VIX alone.
Momentum features
I will calculate recent cumulative returns over short windows such as 5, 10, and 20 trading days. This will allow the models to capture short-run persistence or reversal effects in the stock indices.
Rate-of-change features for macro-financial variables
For example, instead of using only the level of oil prices or Treasury yields, I will also include daily or weekly changes in these variables. Sudden changes may be more informative than levels.
Interaction terms for linear benchmark models
In the linear-model comparison stage, I will test interactions such as VIX × credit spread and yield × USD index to examine whether the effect of one variable depends on the level of another.
Alternative target definitions
I will primarily predict next-day S&P 500 returns, but I will also test:

next-day Dow Jones returns
the sign of the next-day return (up/down classification)
5-day ahead cumulative returns

These alternative targets will help determine whether macro-financial predictors are more useful for direction prediction or slightly longer forecast horizons.




Planned models for the final project

1. Baseline benchmark models
These models will provide a reference point for judging whether more complex methods actually improve predictive performance.

Mean return benchmark
Predict the historical average return for every observation.
Naive zero-return benchmark
Predict that next-day return equals zero.
Lagged linear OLS benchmark
Use the same regression structure as in the midterm as a baseline linear model.



2. Finance benchmark
I may include a CAPM-style market benchmark (a model of expected return in finance theory), but only as a conceptual comparison rather than as the main model, because the project is about predicting market index returns from macro-financial variables rather than explaining asset returns cross-sectionally. Since CAPM is more suitable for individual asset excess returns relative to the market, it is not the most natural primary model for this question. However, it is still possible to construct a simplified benchmark using the market index and risk-free rate if I decide to compare traditional finance intuition against data-driven predictive methods.


3. Tree-based models
These models are especially important because the midterm results suggest the relationships may be nonlinear.

Decision Tree Regressor
Random Forest Regressor
Gradient Boosting Regressor
XGBoost or LightGBM if available in the final setup

These models can capture threshold effects and nonlinear interactions, such as volatility mattering only above certain levels or credit spreads becoming important during stress periods.


4. Classification models
If I include a directional target (positive vs negative next-day return), I will test:

Logistic Regression
Random Forest Classifier
Gradient Boosting Classifier
XGBoost Classifier

This will allow me to examine whether macro-financial variables predict the direction of returns better than the exact return value.




Final evaluation and answering the research question
The final step of the project will be to evaluate whether ML models can meaningfully improve prediction of stock market returns using macro-financial indicators. After constructing the expanded feature set and training the proposed models, each model will be evaluated using out-of-sample test data and time-consistent validation methods.
Model performance will be compared using several metrics, including RMSE, MAE, and out-of-sample \(R^2\) for regression models, as well as accuracy and ROC-AUC if classification models are used to predict the direction of returns. Each model will also be compared against the benchmark models.
Depending on the performance of these models, the central research question will be addressed. If the models substantially outperform the benchmark models and demonstrate stable predictive performance on unseen data, this would suggest that macro-financial indicators contain useful predictive information for short-term stock market returns. Conversely, if even flexible ML models fail to meaningfully improve prediction accuracy, the results would support the conclusion that daily stock market returns are largely unpredictable using the available macro-financial indicators.

title: About
text:
About the Author
My name is Jack Li, and I am an undergraduate student at the University of Toronto pursuing an Honours Bachelor of Science. I am specializing in Data Science, with a focus on quantitative finance, and Mathematical Applications in Economics and Finance.
My academic interests include data analysis, machine learning, statistical modeling, financial economics, and mathematical finance. I have worked with tools such as Python, R, SQL, Pandas, NumPy, Scikit-learn, SciPy, Matplotlib, Tableau, and Git.


Project Context
This website was created for the JSC370 Final Project. The project examines whether macro-financial indicators can meaningfully predict future S&P 500 returns using regression and classification models.


Links

Home
Final Report
Interactive Visualizations
Download the PDF Report
GitHub Repository

title: Interactive Visualizations
text: This page presents three interactive visualizations created from the final project dataset. These figures explore how volatility, return distributions, and market regimes relate to future S&P 500 returns. Each plot includes hover information, and users can zoom or pan within the plot area.

title: Interactive Visualizations
section: Figure 1. VIX and Future 20-Day S&P 500 Returns
text:
Figure 1. VIX and Future 20-Day S&P 500 Returns


                            
                                            


The scatterplot shows that most observations are clustered at relatively low to moderate VIX values, roughly between 10 and 30, with future 20-day returns mostly concentrated around zero. As VIX increases, the spread of future returns becomes wider, meaning that high-volatility periods are associated with more uncertain future outcomes. The fitted trendline slopes slightly upward, but the points are widely scattered around it, so the relationship is weak. This supports the project’s main finding: volatility contains some information about future market conditions, but it is not strong enough by itself to accurately predict future S&P 500 returns.

title: Interactive Visualizations
section: Figure 2. Distribution of Future Returns by Forecast Horizon
text:
Figure 2. Distribution of Future Returns by Forecast Horizon


                            
                                            


This interactive overlay histogram compares future S&P 500 returns across the 1-day, 5-day, and 20-day forecast horizons. The dashed vertical line marks a zero return. The plot shows that return distributions become wider as the forecast horizon increases, which helps explain why exact return prediction becomes difficult. The future returns are centered around zero across all three forecast horizons, but the spread becomes wider as the horizon increases. The 1-day returns are tightly concentrated near zero, which means most short-term market movements are small. The 5-day distribution is wider, and the 20-day distribution is the widest, showing that longer horizons create a larger range of possible future outcomes. This helps explain why the regression models struggled to predict exact return size: even though average returns are close to zero, the realized outcomes vary substantially, especially at the 20-day horizon.

title: Interactive Visualizations
section: Figure 3. Future 20-Day Returns by VIX Regime
text:
Figure 3. Future 20-Day Returns by VIX Regime


                            
                                            


This interactive boxplot compares future 20-day S&P 500 returns across four VIX regimes. The regimes are created by dividing the VIX into four groups from low to very high volatility. The plot shows how the distribution of future returns changes when market volatility is low, moderate, high, or very high. The spread of future 20-day returns becomes wider as the VIX regime increases. Low VIX periods have a relatively narrow range of future returns, while very high VIX periods show much larger positive and negative outcomes. This suggests that high-volatility periods are associated with greater uncertainty about future market movement. The median return appears slightly positive across the regimes, but the very high VIX group has many extreme observations in both directions. This supports the project’s broader finding that volatility is informative about market conditions, but it does not make future returns easy to predict because high volatility increases the range of possible outcomes.

title: Interactive Visualizations
section: Figure 4. VIX, Credit Spread, and Future 20-Day S&P 500 Return
text:
Figure 4. VIX, Credit Spread, and Future 20-Day S&P 500 Return


                            
                                            


This interactive 3D scatterplot shows the relationship between the VIX, credit spread, and future 20-day S&P 500 return. The x-axis shows market volatility, the y-axis shows credit-market stress, and the z-axis shows the future 20-day return. Points are colored by actual return direction, with one color representing positive future returns and the other representing non-positive future returns. We see that most observations are concentrated at lower VIX values and lower credit spreads, where future 20-day returns are clustered close to zero. When VIX and credit spreads are higher, the points become more spread out vertically, so that future returns become more uncertain. However the general trend as VIX increases is that the future returns become higher and the credit spread of the non-positive returns increases. The positive and non-positive return points also overlap heavily, which shows why classification performance is only modest. Even though volatility and credit spreads provide useful information about market conditions, they do not cleanly separate positive and negative future returns. This supports the project’s main conclusion that macro-financial indicators contain some predictive signal, especially for 20-day direction, but the signal is not strong enough to produce highly accurate forecasts.

title: Project Overview
text:
Financial markets respond to a wide range of economic signals, including interest rates, inflation, labor market conditions, investor sentiment, commodity prices, and volatility. This project examines whether these publicly available macro-financial indicators contain useful information for predicting future S&P 500 returns.
The central research question is:
Can macro-financial indicators meaningfully predict future S&P 500 returns, and do machine learning methods improve predictive accuracy relative to simpler benchmark and linear models?
To answer this question, the project combines daily financial market data from Yahoo Finance with macroeconomic indicators from Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED). The analysis evaluates two related prediction tasks:

Regression: predicting the numerical size of future S&P 500 returns

Classification: predicting whether future S&P 500 returns are positive or non-positive

Both tasks are evaluated across 1-day, 5-day, and 20-day forecast horizons.


The dataset combines market-based indicators with broader macroeconomic measures. These variables were chosen to capture different channels through which financial and economic conditions may affect equity returns.
Key variables include:

Equity market indicators: S&P 500 Index and Dow Jones Industrial Average
Interest-rate indicators: 10-year Treasury yield, 1-year Treasury yield, and Federal Funds Rate
Risk and uncertainty indicators: CBOE Volatility Index and credit spread
Macroeconomic indicators: Consumer Price Index, unemployment rate, industrial production, and consumer sentiment
Market condition indicators: U.S. Dollar Index and crude oil prices

The final modeling dataset also includes engineered predictors such as lagged variables, rolling averages, rolling volatility, momentum measures, short-run changes, and yield-spread features.



The project uses a chronological training / validation / test split to reflect the structure of a real forecasting problem. The models are trained on earlier observations, tuned using a later validation period, and evaluated on a final held-out test period. This avoids random shuffling and reduces look-ahead bias.
The model comparison includes:

Benchmark models: historical mean forecast and majority-class classifier
Linear models: Ordinary Least Squares and Logistic Regression
Regularized models: Ridge Regression and Lasso Regression
Machine learning models: Random Forest and XGBoost

Hyperparameters were tuned using Optuna. Regression models were evaluated using RMSE, MAE, and out-of-sample \(R^2\). Classification models were evaluated using accuracy and ROC-AUC.



The results suggest that predicting the exact size of future S&P 500 returns is highly difficult. Most regression models produced out-of-sample \(R^2\) values close to zero or negative. The strongest regression result came from the 1-day Random Forest model, but its improvement over the benchmark was very small, suggesting limited practical predictive power.
Classification results were somewhat more promising, but still modest. Logistic Regression performed best across all three forecast horizons based on ROC-AUC, with the strongest result at the 20-day horizon. This suggests that macro-financial indicators may contain some information about medium-term market direction, even though the signal is not strong enough to support highly accurate forecasting.
The model interpretation results also show that different prediction tasks rely on different types of information. Short-term return prediction was driven mainly by volatility and momentum variables, while the 20-day direction model placed more weight on broader macro-financial indicators such as interest rates, unemployment, inflation, oil prices, and credit spreads.
Overall, the findings support a mixed conclusion: macro-financial indicators provide little evidence of meaningful predictability for exact return size, but they show limited usefulness for classifying medium-term market direction.

title: JSC370 Final Report
text:
Financial markets are shaped by a combination of macroeconomic conditions, investor expectations, and changing risk sentiment. Researchers, policymakers, and investors have long been interested in whether publicly observable economic and financial indicators contain useful information about future stock market performance. Variables such as interest rates, inflation, unemployment, credit spreads, commodity prices, and market volatility may reflect underlying economic strength or financial stress, and therefore may help explain movements in equity markets.
At the same time, predicting daily stock returns remains one of the most difficult tasks in empirical finance. Stock prices respond rapidly to new information, investor behavior, and unexpected shocks, which often makes short-horizon returns highly noisy and difficult to forecast. While some macro-financial indicators may exhibit statistical relationships with returns, it is less clear whether these relationships are strong enough to generate meaningful out-of-sample predictive performance.
This project investigates whether macro-financial indicators can be used to predict future daily returns of major U.S. stock indices. The analysis focuses on the S&P 500, a broad benchmark of the U.S. equity market, and uses a historical dataset combining market-based indicators from Yahoo Finance and macroeconomic variables from the Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED) API. The dataset spans multiple economic regimes, including expansions, recessions, financial crises, and periods of elevated inflation and monetary tightening.
The central research question is:
Can macro-financial indicators meaningfully predict next-day stock market returns, and do machine learning methods improve predictive accuracy relative to traditional linear models?
This question extends the earlier midterm analysis, which found that some lagged variables—particularly market volatility, Treasury yields, and credit spreads—had statistically significant but economically small relationships with next-day returns. Those findings suggest that predictive signals may exist, but simple linear models explain only a very small share of daily return variation. This raises the possibility that the relevant relationships may be nonlinear, state-dependent, or only detectable when many variables are combined using more flexible methods.
To address this question, the final project will compare multiple predictive approaches. First, benchmark models such as historical-average forecasts and ordinary least squares regression will be estimated. Second, regularized linear models such as Ridge and Lasso regression will be used to improve feature selection and reduce overfitting. Third, machine learning models such as Random Forest and XGBoost will be used to capture nonlinear interactions and threshold effects. In addition to predicting the numerical value of next-day returns, a secondary analysis will classify whether the market increases or decreases on the following day.
The contribution of this project is therefore twofold. Substantively, it evaluates whether commonly observed macro-financial indicators contain useful short-term predictive information for equity markets. Methodologically, it compares classical statistical models with modern machine learning approaches using a time-series forecasting framework. The results will help determine whether daily stock returns can be meaningfully forecast using publicly available macro-financial data, or whether such returns remain largely unpredictable even with more advanced predictive models.

title: JSC370 Final Report
text:
Data Acquisition
The dataset was constructed from two public data sources: Yahoo Finance and Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED). Daily financial market data were collected from the Yahoo Finance chart API using a custom Python request function. For each ticker symbol, the function sent a request to the Yahoo Finance chart endpoint with a start timestamp, end timestamp, daily interval, and adjusted-close option. The JSON response contained timestamped quote data, including open, high, low, close, adjusted close, and volume. After converting the Unix timestamps into calendar dates, the analysis retained the daily closing-price series for each financial indicator.
The Yahoo Finance series included the S&P 500 Index, Dow Jones Industrial Average, 10-year U.S. Treasury yield, 1-year U.S. Treasury yield, U.S. Dollar Index, CBOE Volatility Index (VIX), and crude oil futures. These variables were selected to capture equity market performance, interest-rate conditions, currency strength, market uncertainty, and commodity price movements.
Macroeconomic data were collected from the FRED API using a custom function that requested observations for each FRED series ID. The JSON output from FRED was converted into a pandas data frame with two main columns: date and value. The FRED series included the Federal Funds Rate, Consumer Price Index (CPI), unemployment rate, industrial production index, University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index, Moody’s BAA corporate bond yield, and the 10-year Treasury yield. In addition, a credit spread variable was constructed as the difference between Moody’s BAA corporate bond yield and the 10-year Treasury yield, representing perceived credit risk and financial stress.
The final merged dataset combines daily financial indicators with lower-frequency macroeconomic variables and spans multiple decades of U.S. economic history, including expansions, recessions, financial crises, inflationary periods, and monetary tightening cycles.

title: JSC370 Final Report
text:
Data Cleaning and Wrangling
Raw market data from Yahoo Finance were converted into structured pandas data frames with standardized date columns and closing-price variables. Only the relevant closing-price series were retained for analysis. All dates were converted to Python datetime format to ensure consistency across sources.
Macroeconomic series from FRED are reported at monthly or lower frequencies, while market data are observed daily. To align these sources, lower-frequency macroeconomic variables were resampled to daily frequency using forward-fill interpolation, allowing the most recently available macroeconomic observation to remain in effect until a new release date.
After the individual series were cleaned and renamed, all variables were merged by date using the date index. The merged dataset was then restricted to rows where all selected variables were available. Remaining missing values were removed after the merge and again after feature engineering, because lagged variables, rolling means, rolling volatility, and future return targets naturally create missing observations at the beginning and end of the sample. This process ensured that all models were trained on complete observations with the same predictor set.
Daily stock returns were computed using percentage changes in index closing prices. The primary dependent variable is the next-period S&P 500 return, while secondary targets include Dow Jones returns and binary direction indicators equal to one when the future return is positive and zero otherwise.
To avoid look-ahead bias, predictor variables were lagged so that only information available at time \(t\) is used to predict returns at time \(t+1\), \(t+5\), or \(t+20\).

title: JSC370 Final Report
text:
Feature Engineering
To improve predictive performance beyond the original midterm specification, the final project extends the dataset through feature engineering. In addition to one-day lagged predictors, the following features were constructed:

Multiple lag variables (1-day, 3-day, 5-day, and 10-day lags)
Rolling means over 5, 10, and 20 trading days
Rolling volatility measures of recent stock returns
Momentum features based on cumulative past returns
First differences and short-run changes in yields, oil prices, volatility, and spreads
Yield-curve spread variables (10-year minus 1-year Treasury yield)

These engineered variables are intended to capture delayed reactions, short-term trends, volatility regimes, and changing macro-financial conditions.

title: JSC370 Final Report
text:
Exploratory Data Analysis
Initial data exploration was conducted using pandas, matplotlib, seaborn, and plotnine (ggplot for Python). Publication-quality figures were created to summarize historical patterns and motivate the predictive modeling stage.
Exploratory visualizations included:

Time-series plots of major stock indices
Time-series plots of the VIX and other financial indicators
Histograms of daily stock returns
Scatterplots of returns against macro-financial predictors
Correlation heatmaps across all variables
Tables summarizing periods of elevated volatility

These analyses were used to assess distributional shape, outliers, temporal trends, and potential predictor relationships.
Here is a sample of the data distribution of finalized daily S&P 500 returns:

title: JSC370 Final Report
text:
Statistical Tests and Baseline Analysis
Several classical statistical methods were used in the midterm stage and retained as baseline evidence for the final project.
First, ordinary least squares (OLS) regression was used to estimate the linear relationship between lagged macro-financial indicators and next-day S&P 500 returns.
Second, two-sample t-tests were used to compare mean stock returns during high-volatility periods (e.g., VIX > 40) versus normal market conditions.
Third, Pearson correlation analysis was used to examine pairwise linear relationships between lagged predictors and future returns.
These methods provide interpretable benchmarks and help assess whether any predictive signal exists prior to more flexible machine learning models.

title: JSC370 Final Report
text:
Predictive Modeling
The primary objective of the final project is forecasting future stock returns using supervised learning methods. Two prediction tasks are considered:

Regression: predict numerical future returns

Classification: predict whether future returns are positive or negative

Models are estimated for three forecasting horizons:

1 trading day ahead
5 trading days ahead
20 trading days ahead


Tier 1: Benchmark Models

Historical mean return forecast
Majority-class classifier
Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)



Tier 2: Regularized Linear Models

Ridge Regression
Lasso Regression
Logistic Regression



Tier 3: Machine Learning Models

Random Forest
XGBoost (if computationally feasible)

These models were selected to compare traditional linear approaches with nonlinear tree-based methods capable of capturing interactions and threshold effects.

title: JSC370 Final Report
text:
Hyperparameter Tuning
For models with tunable hyperparameters, optimization is performed using Optuna. Separate objective functions are defined for each model type.

For regression models, validation-set RMSE is minimized.
For classification models, validation-set ROC-AUC is maximized.

Optuna searches across predefined parameter ranges using parallel trials. After tuning, the best model specification is refit on the combined training and validation data.

title: JSC370 Final Report
text:
Training, Validation, and Testing Procedure
Because the dataset is time-series data, observations were not randomly shuffled. Random splitting would introduce look-ahead bias by allowing future information to enter the training set.
Instead, the sample was divided chronologically:

Training set (70%): used to estimate models

Validation set (15%): used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection

Test set (15%): reserved for final out-of-sample evaluation

This framework more accurately reflects real forecasting conditions, where only past information is available when making predictions.

title: JSC370 Final Report
text:
Evaluation Metrics
For regression models, forecasting performance is evaluated using:

Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
Out-of-sample \(R^2\)

For classification models, predictive performance is evaluated using:

Accuracy
Precision / Recall (if relevant)
Receiver Operating Characteristic Area Under Curve (ROC-AUC)

These metrics allow comparison across models and forecast horizons.

title: JSC370 Final Report
text:
Training, Validation, and Testing Procedure
Because the data are time-series observations, the dataset should not be split randomly. Random shuffling would mix future observations into the training set and create look-ahead bias, leading to overly optimistic model performance. Instead, the data should be split chronologically so that models are always trained on past information and evaluated on future periods.
The engineered dataset model_df is therefore divided into training, validation, and test sets based on time order:

Training set: used to estimate model parameters

Validation set: used for model tuning and model selection

Test set: held out until the end for final out-of-sample evaluation

This setup more closely reflects the real forecasting problem faced by investors, where only historical information is available when making predictions.

Table 2. Regression Performance Summary
Table 2 shows that the regression models had weak out-of-sample performance across all three forecast horizons. At the 1-day horizon, Random Forest performed best, with an RMSE of 0.01140 and a small positive \(R^2\) of 0.00384. This means it slightly improved over the mean benchmark, whose \(R^2\) was -0.00045, but the improvement was extremely small. XGBoost and Lasso were very close to the mean benchmark, with \(R^2\) values of -0.00013 and -0.00017, respectively. At the 5-day horizon, XGBoost performed best with an \(R^2\) of -0.00083, followed closely by Lasso at -0.00091. These values are still slightly negative, showing almost no meaningful predictive gain over the benchmark. At the 20-day horizon, the mean benchmark performed best, with an \(R^2\) of -0.01352, while all fitted models performed worse. XGBoost performed especially poorly at this horizon, with an \(R^2\) of -1.43134. Overall, the results suggest that the engineered macro-financial indicators provide very limited ability to predict the exact size of future S&P 500 returns. The only positive \(R^2\) appears for the 1-day Random Forest model, but the value is so close to zero that it should be interpreted as a very weak improvement rather than strong predictive power. In relation to the research question, this table suggests that machine learning models provide little improvement for predicting exact return size, even when many macro-financial predictors are included.

Table 3. Classification Performance Summary
Table 3 shows that the classification models had modest performance overall, with the strongest results coming from Logistic Regression. At the 1-day horizon, Logistic Regression performed best, with an accuracy of 0.54304 and a ROC-AUC of 0.53035. This is slightly better than random classification, but the improvement is small. XGBoost also had a reasonable 1-day accuracy of 0.53241, but its ROC-AUC was only 0.50967, suggesting weak ranking ability between positive and non-positive return days.
At the 5-day horizon, classification performance was weaker. Logistic Regression had the highest ROC-AUC among the fitted models, but its value was 0.49491, which is slightly below the random benchmark of 0.50. XGBoost and Random Forest also performed below 0.50, with ROC-AUC values of 0.46970 and 0.42498. The majority-class benchmark had the highest accuracy at 0.58342, but this does not necessarily mean it was a better predictive model. It mainly reflects the fact that one class was more common in the 5-day test set.
At the 20-day horizon, Logistic Regression again performed best by ROC-AUC, reaching 0.57166. This is the strongest classification result in the table and suggests some ability to distinguish positive from non-positive 20-day returns. However, its accuracy was only 0.39320, while the majority-class benchmark had an accuracy of 0.64081. This shows why ROC-AUC is more useful than accuracy here: accuracy is strongly affected by class imbalance, while ROC-AUC evaluates the model’s ranking ability across different thresholds. The classification results suggest that directional prediction is still weak, but the 20-day Logistic Regression model shows the clearest predictive signal.
Moreover, these results suggest that direction classification is somewhat more promising than return-size prediction, but the improvement over random classification is still modest.

Table 5. Best Classification Model by Forecast Horizon
Tables 4 and 5 summarize the best-performing model at each forecast horizon. For regression, the best model changes across horizons. Random Forest performs best for the 1-day horizon, with an RMSE of 0.01140 and a small positive \(R^2\) of 0.00384. XGBoost performs best for the 5-day horizon, with an RMSE of 0.02427 and an \(R^2\) of -0.00083. At the 20-day horizon, the mean benchmark performs best, with an RMSE of 0.04529 and an \(R^2\) of -0.01352. This pattern shows that no single regression model consistently dominates across all horizons. More importantly, even the best regression results are very close to zero or negative, meaning that the models provide little meaningful improvement for predicting exact return size.
For classification, Logistic Regression is the best model across all three horizons based on ROC-AUC. Its ROC-AUC is 0.53035 at the 1-day horizon, 0.49491 at the 5-day horizon, and 0.57166 at the 20-day horizon. The 5-day value is slightly below 0.50, indicating that even the best model at this horizon performs no better than random classification. The strongest result appears at the 20-day horizon, where Logistic Regression reaches a ROC-AUC of 0.57166. Overall, these tables show that model performance depends on both the prediction task and forecast horizon. The clearest predictive signal appears in 20-day direction classification, while exact return prediction remains weak.

Table 8. XGBoost Hyperparameters
Tables 6 through 8 report the tuned hyperparameters selected through Optuna for the regularized and tree-based models. For Ridge regression, the selected alpha values are large across all horizons, ranging from 89.64764 to 99.87613. This suggests that the model preferred stronger regularization, likely because the engineered predictor set contains many related variables and could otherwise overfit the training data. For Random Forest, the selected settings vary across regression and classification tasks. For example, the number of trees ranges from 106 to 348, and maximum depth ranges from 2 to 20, showing that different horizons required different levels of model complexity. For XGBoost, the tuned number of estimators ranges from 190 to 705, with learning rates between 0.00882 and 0.04640. These differences show that the models were not trained using one fixed setting, but were separately optimized for each task and forecast horizon using the validation set.

Table 10. Friedman Test for Classification Performance Across Horizons
Tables 9 and 10 test whether model performance differs systematically across the 1-day, 5-day, and 20-day forecast horizons. For regression performance, the Friedman test is statistically significant, with a test statistic of 12.00 and a p-value of 0.00248. This means there is evidence that regression performance changes across forecast horizons. This matches the earlier model results, where performance was relatively less weak at the 1-day horizon but became worse at longer horizons, especially for several fitted models at the 20-day horizon.
For classification performance, the Friedman test is also statistically significant, with a test statistic of 6.00 and a p-value of 0.04979. This suggests that ROC-AUC performance also differs across horizons at the 5% level, although the result is very close to the cutoff. The classification tables show that the 20-day horizon has the strongest ROC-AUC result, especially for Logistic Regression, while the 5-day horizon performs weakest. These tests therefore support the idea that forecast horizon matters for both regression and classification, but the classification result should be interpreted cautiously because the p-value is only slightly below 0.05.

title: JSC370 Final Report
text:
Regression Performance

Best Out-of-Sample R² by Horizon









Figure 1. Best Out-of-Sample \(R^2\) by Forecast Horizon
Figure 1 shows the best out-of-sample \(R^2\) value at each forecast horizon. At the 1-day horizon, Random Forest performs best, with a small positive \(R^2\) of 0.00384. This means it slightly improves over the mean benchmark, but the improvement is very small. At the 5-day horizon, XGBoost performs best, but its \(R^2\) is still slightly negative at -0.00083, indicating almost no meaningful predictive gain. At the 20-day horizon, the mean benchmark is the best-performing approach, with an \(R^2\) of -0.01352. Overall, the figure shows that exact return prediction is weakest at longer horizons and that even the best regression results are close to zero or negative. The only positive result is the 1-day Random Forest model, but its value is too small to suggest strong predictive power. This means that the predictive modeling framework does not produce strong evidence of numerical return predictability. ## Classification Performance


Best ROC-AUC by Horizon









Figure 2. Best ROC-AUC by Forecast Horizon
Figure 2 shows that Logistic Regression achieved the highest ROC-AUC across the forecast horizons, but the results remain close to the random-classification benchmark of \(\text{ROC-AUC} = 0.50\). The best performance occurs at the 20-day horizon, with \(\text{ROC-AUC} \approx 0.57\), while the 1-day horizon reaches about \(\text{ROC-AUC} \approx 0.53\) and the 5-day horizon is almost exactly \(\text{ROC-AUC} \approx 0.50\). This suggests that the model has weak predictive ability overall, although it performs slightly better when predicting longer-term market direction. This suggests that the macro-financial variables are more useful for ranking future market direction than for predicting exact return values, especially at the 20-day horizon.


Regression Model Comparison Heatmap









Figure 3. Regression Model Comparison Heatmap: Out-of-Sample \(R^2\)
Figure 3 compares the out-of-sample \(R^2\) values across different regression models and forecast horizons. Overall, the results suggest that the regression models have weak explanatory power for future market returns, since the out-of-sample \(R^2\) values are close to zero or negative in most cases. A negative out-of-sample \(R^2\) means that the model performs worse than a simple benchmark prediction, such as using the historical average return. Although some model and horizon combinations perform slightly better than others, the differences appear small. This suggests that predicting the exact size of future returns is difficult, even when using multiple market and macroeconomic indicators.


Classification Model Comparison Heatmap









Figure 4. Classification Model Comparison Heatmap: ROC-AUC
Figure 4 compares the ROC-AUC performance of the classification models across the 1-day, 5-day, and 20-day forecast horizons. Overall, Logistic Regression performs the best among the three models, especially at the 20-day horizon, where it reaches the highest ROC-AUC value of \(\text{ROC-AUC} = 0.5717\). At the 1-day horizon, Logistic Regression also performs the best with \(\text{ROC-AUC} = 0.5303\), compared with XGBoost at \(\text{ROC-AUC} = 0.5097\) and Random Forest at \(\text{ROC-AUC} = 0.4927\). However, all models perform poorly at the 5-day horizon, with ROC-AUC values below \(0.50\) or very close to it. This means that the models have little ability to distinguish between positive and negative market movements at that horizon. Overall, the classification results suggest that predicting market direction is difficult, but Logistic Regression provides the most consistent performance, especially for the longer 20-day forecast horizon.

title: JSC370 Final Report
text:
Prediction Diagnostics

Actual vs Predicted Returns









Figure 5. Actual vs Predicted Returns for the Best Overall Regression Model









Figure 6. Predicted Probabilities by Actual Class for the Best Overall Classification Model
Figure 5 shows the actual versus predicted returns for the best overall regression model, which is Random Forest at the 1-day forecast horizon. The dashed diagonal line represents perfect prediction, where the predicted return would equal the actual return. However, most predicted returns are clustered very close to \(0\), even when actual returns range from about \(-0.06\) to \(0.095\). This means that the model is not capturing the full size of market movements. Instead, it tends to predict returns near the average, which makes it less useful for forecasting large positive or negative changes. Overall, this supports the earlier out-of-sample \(R^2\) results, suggesting that the regression model has limited predictive power for the magnitude of future returns.
Figure 6 shows the predicted probabilities from the best overall classification model, which is Logistic Regression at the 20-day forecast horizon. The dashed horizontal line at \(0.50\) represents the usual cutoff for classifying a return as positive. Ideally, observations with actual positive returns should have predicted probabilities mostly above \(0.50\), while observations with actual non-positive returns should have probabilities mostly below \(0.50\). However, the two groups overlap heavily. Many actual positive-return cases still receive probabilities below \(0.50\), and some non-positive-return cases receive probabilities above \(0.50\). This shows that the model has some ability to rank positive and non-positive outcomes, but the separation between the two classes is weak. This is consistent with the ROC-AUC value of about \(0.57\), which is better than random guessing but still limited.


ROC Curve for Best Classifier









Figure 7. ROC Curve (Logistic Regression, 20-Day Direction)
Figure 7 shows the ROC curve for the best overall classification model, Logistic Regression at the 20-day forecast horizon. The model has an AUC of approximately 0.572, which is above the random-classifier benchmark of 0.50. This means the model has some ability to rank positive 20-day return cases above non-positive return cases. However, the curve remains fairly close to the diagonal line, so the improvement is modest rather than strong. This figure supports the classification results in Table 3: the clearest directional signal appears at the 20-day horizon, but the model’s predictive power is still limited.

title: JSC370 Final Report
text:
Feature Importance and Model Interpretation









Figure 8. Random Forest Regressor Feature Importance for 1-Day Return Prediction
Figure 8 shows the most important predictors used by the Random Forest model for 1-day S&P 500 return prediction. The top predictors are mainly volatility and momentum variables. The most important feature is the 1-day change in the VIX, followed by the 5-day change in the VIX, 20-day Dow Jones momentum, the VIX level itself, and shorter-term S&P 500 and Dow Jones momentum measures. This suggests that the Random Forest model relied more on recent market sentiment, volatility changes, and price momentum than on slower-moving macroeconomic indicators. For example, variables such as unemployment, inflation, and industrial production do not appear among the top features, while VIX-related and momentum variables dominate the ranking. However, these importance values should be interpreted as model-based associations rather than causal effects. They show which variables the model used most for prediction, not which variables directly caused future returns.









Figure 9. Logistic Regression Coefficients for 20-Day Direction Prediction
Figure 9 shows the largest standardized coefficients from the Logistic Regression model for 20-day direction prediction. The strongest positive coefficient is the 20-day rolling average of the 1-year Treasury yield, which is much larger than the other coefficients. This means that, within this fitted model, higher recent short-term yield levels are associated with a higher predicted probability of a positive 20-day S&P 500 return. Other positive coefficients include the federal funds rate, the unemployment rate, lagged inflation, lagged unemployment, 20-day Dow Jones volatility, and the 20-day rolling credit spread. Negative coefficients include the 20-day rolling oil price, lagged unemployment, lagged industrial production, lagged federal funds rate, the 20-day rolling USD Index, 20-day S&P 500 volatility, and several rolling yield measures.
Overall, this figure suggests that the 20-day direction model relies more on broader macro-financial conditions than the 1-day Random Forest return model. Unlike Figure 8, where volatility and momentum variables dominate, Figure 9 includes more interest-rate, oil-price, unemployment, inflation, industrial-production, and credit-spread variables. However, these coefficients should be interpreted as model-based associations rather than causal effects. They show how the Logistic Regression model used standardized predictors to classify future market direction, not whether these variables directly caused future returns.
This helps answer the research question by showing that the limited predictive signal comes mainly from volatility, momentum, and interest-rate-related variables rather than from all macroeconomic indicators equally.
Although XGBoost was included in the model comparison, its feature-importance plot was not retained because the fitted model did not produce a readable nonzero importance ranking for the selected 1-day return model. To avoid presenting a misleading blank figure, the interpretation focuses on Random Forest feature importance and Logistic Regression coefficients, which provide clearer information about the predictors used by the models.
